repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem

When running nvim on a remote machine over SSH, if there is high ping,
then bg detection may not complete in time. This results in a warning
every time nvim is started. #38648

## Solution

Restore `ttyfast` option and allow it to control whether or not bg
detection is performed. Because this is during startup and before any
user config or commands, we use the environment variable
`NVIM_NOTTYFAST` to allow disabling `ttyfast` during initialization.

comment_data.body:
```suggestion
          "defaults.lua: Did not detect DSR response from terminal for 'background' detection. This results in a slower startup time. To disable this and other 'ttyfast' features during startup, set the environment variable NVIM_NOTTYFAST",
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -991,7 +994,7 @@ do
         and os.getenv('NVIM_TEST') == nil
       then
         vim.notify(
-          'defaults.lua: Did not detect DSR response from terminal. This results in a slower startup time.',
+          'defaults.lua: Did not detect DSR response from terminal for `background` detection. This results in a slower startup time. To disable this and other `ttyfast` features during startup, set the environment variable `NVIM_NOTTYFAST`',

comment_data.body:
```suggestion
        Assume that the underlying terminal can respond quickly to queries
        required by features such as 'background' detection.
        
        Nvim issues terminal queries before reading the user's |config| file,
        so disabling this option there will not work. Set $NVIM_NOTTYFAST
        before starting Nvim to disable terminal queries.
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -9837,12 +9837,19 @@ local options = {
     {
       abbreviation = 'tf',
       defaults = true,
+      desc = [=[
+        Assume that the underlying terminal can respond quickly to support
+        features such as background detection. In slow scenarios like nvim over
+        SSH with high latency, you may wish to disable this option during
+        startup by setting the environment variable `NVIM_NOTTYFAST`. This
+        allows it to take effect before executing your config.

comment_data.body:
This condition should also check that `'ttyfast'` is enabled, since it too issues a query.



This will help resolve https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/28776
```suggestion
      elseif (colorterm == nil or colorterm == '') and vim.o.ttyfast then
```
Thanks, I struggled with wording there.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -9837,12 +9837,19 @@ local options = {
     {
       abbreviation = 'tf',
       defaults = true,
+      desc = [=[
+        Assume that the underlying terminal can respond quickly to support
+        features such as background detection. In slow scenarios like nvim over
+        SSH with high latency, you may wish to disable this option during
+        startup by setting the environment variable `NVIM_NOTTYFAST`. This
+        allows it to take effect before executing your config.

comment_data.body: (Random nit, just curious): isn't it possible in the future that we deal with more OSC codes? In this case I think removing the `'background'` specification would be a bit more future-proof and less verbose.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -991,7 +994,7 @@ do
         and os.getenv('NVIM_TEST') == nil
       then
         vim.notify(
-          'defaults.lua: Did not detect DSR response from terminal. This results in a slower startup time.',
+          "defaults.lua: Did not detect DSR response from terminal for 'background' detection. This results in a slower startup time. To disable this and other 'ttyfast' features during startup, set the environment variable NVIM_NOTTYFAST",

comment_data.body: I dunno, maybe we'll deal with more in the future. There are a few other places that use a sentinel response (e.g. DA1) to determine capabilities, but it's all scattered right now. The more verbose message may help a user understand the implications of disabling that variable, and it's easy to change the message if more stuff is added later.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -991,7 +994,7 @@ do
         and os.getenv('NVIM_TEST') == nil
       then
         vim.notify(
-          'defaults.lua: Did not detect DSR response from terminal. This results in a slower startup time.',
+          "defaults.lua: Did not detect DSR response from terminal for 'background' detection. This results in a slower startup time. To disable this and other 'ttyfast' features during startup, set the environment variable NVIM_NOTTYFAST",

comment_regular_data.body:
Thanks for your effort here. But env vars aren't the right approach. In the worst case, we can:

- document how to delete the autocmd that does this work,
    - assign a dedicated augroup so that the user can surgically remove only that handler?
- and/or move the logic to somewhere like `runtime/lua/vim/tty.lua`, and the user can nuke it via `tty.foo = function()end`.

So those are near-term steps we can take. 

The ideal/long-term approach is to either detect the conditions where we shouldn't wait (depends on if we found any such conditions in https://github.com/neovim/neovim/discussions/38648 ), or increase the wait.

We could also consider restoring 'ttyfast', and if that option is disabled then we could change behavior for things like this.

- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/1045
This is done in defaults.lua directly, not in an autocommand. All of defaults.lua has already executed when user config is sourced.

> document how to delete the autocmd that does this work

> and/or move the logic to somewhere like `runtime/lua/vim/tty.lua`, and the user can nuke it via `tty.foo = function()end`.

> We could also consider restoring 'ttyfast', and if that option is disabled then we could change behavior for things like this.

Like @zeertzjq said, this executes directly in `defaults.lua` and waits to complete before processing config, so I don't think any of these will work.

> The ideal/long-term approach is to either detect the conditions where we shouldn't wait (depends on if we found any such conditions in #38648 ), or increase the wait.

If we just look at the ssh example, I'm not sure how we can detect it. It's just nvim running on a remote machine in any environment. If I ssh into a server with 500 ping, then the `vim.wait` could last the whole time, time out, and then allow startup to continue (also generating a warning). This would add a 100 ms delay to startup every time, and if we increase the wait, it only gets worse. I can see some users opting for no delay even if it means some flickering or minor syntax highlighting bugs.

One option is to move the logic into a function like `tty.bg_detect()` and force the user to run it in their own config early on to detect the bg before processing the rest of their config. I'm not sure if this could bring back some of the bugs in syntax highlighting due to the change in sequence, and this would be a breaking change.

I guess another option is just to remove the warning.

Still looking for the root cause for the user that wasn't using ssh, but at least for ssh, I think it makes sense to make it optional.

Sorry for missing that detail. If it happens before user config, then the approaches we have are:

- ~~`--cmd`~~
- environment variable
- decide internally based on `$TERM`, etc.

right? so initially, we could restore 'ttyfast' and use can do `nvim --cmd 'set nottyfast'` to disable the wait (which also disables the bg detection).

`$TERM` is just relying on another environment variable, but that also doesn't solve the ssh latency problem because they can be using any terminal over ssh (unless we instruct users to set `$TERM` to something special to indicate it's ssh, which will break other programs).

If we really don't want to use environment variables, we could try something like:
- Send DSR
- Wait up to 5ms for response
- If we don't get a response in time, assume high latency environment or DSR unsupported
  - In this case, we can still send the bg query but skip the `vim.wait`
- If we do get a response in time, we're in a supported environment
  - In this case, send bg query and use another `vim.wait()` to ensure BG is set properly before processing user config

This leads to a max startup delay of about 5ms if DSR isn't answered. However, in those cases, it again leads to the UI bugs that the `vim.wait()` resolved, with the user having no choice over it.
Running `--cmd` before defaults makes no sense. The main purpose of defaults is moving initialization code from C to Lua. If `--cmd` runs before them they aren't initialization code anymore.

This discussion makes me think no action is needed. If users have to set environment variables to say "I have a broken / poorly-performing environment", that is not much better than just *waiting 100ms*.  Skipping the warning is just hiding a problem. If the environment is that slow then clearly the user expects things to be slow.


Asking users to set `NVIM_NO_BG_WAIT` is just clumsy, and won't solve the problem for 95% of users who aren't going to care enough to fiddle with this. OTOH, a "generic" setting like 'nottyfast' is acceptable because it's a single concept that gathers all the clumsiness in one place.


The only open question is whether there are some known-bad scenarios that we can detect, which tell us to skip detection+warning.

I'm a fan of resurrecting `'ttyfast'`, since Nvim has added a number of queries (not just bg detection) since that option was deprecated, so having a single option that we can use to disable those queries (or do something else like increase timeout) makes sense.
Unfortunately we will need a `--nottyfast` or `NVIM_NOTTYFAST` option since `--cmd` [runs after defaults.lua](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38699#issuecomment-4186440216).

I don't know of any way we could detect by default that a user is in a slow setup (SSH with high latency), but I think a `nottyfast` option would suffice and be less clunky than what I have here. @justinmk Do you have a preference for env var vs cmdline flag? I think env var is nice because it's easily inherited in nvim terminals.

I could take a stab at it, but I'd probably keep the behavior changes scoped to the bg detection for now because I don't know the other areas we may want to change. Do you think we should skip the bg detection altogether if nottyfast is set?

> Do you think we should skip the bg detection altogether if nottyfast is set?

yes 👍 

> Do you have a preference for env var vs cmdline flag?

doesn't matter. they are both horrible 😂
Alright. I repurposed this MR for ttyfast. I agree it's kind of nasty, but do with it what you want.
This wasn't AI assisted. It may have been labelled as such because I borked my branch temporarily.

title: feat(startup): restore ttyfast to control startup behavior
